questions.prompt: Why are smaller PRs better than massive ones?

choices.text: Massive PRs (1000+ lines) get skimmed instead of read carefully; reviewers can't hold all context. Bugs slip through. Smaller PRs (under ~400 lines) get thorough review, faster turnaround, fewer conflicts with main, easier rollback if something's wrong. The discipline of breaking changes apart pays back continuously: separate refactor PRs from feature PRs; separate tests from logic; ship behind feature flags and activate later. PR size is a leading indicator of code review quality and team velocity.

questions.explanation: Big PRs get skimmed; small PRs get reviewed. Bugs slip through skimmed reviews.

questions.prompt: Team has no PR template; PRs have varying quality of context. What to add?

choices.text: Add `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with sections like: Summary (one sentence), Why (link to ticket/design doc), What changed (bullet list), Testing (how you verified), Risks (what could break). Every new PR auto-populates this template; authors fill in; reviewers know exactly what to expect. Eliminates 'what's this PR even about' confusion that wastes review time. Combine with branch protection requiring linked issues and CI passing — turns implicit norms into platform-enforced process. Standard part of mature GitHub repos.

questions.explanation: .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md auto-populates every PR with the right sections.
